step.title: Step 3
step.decisionPreview: predict(expect_change=True, note='RIGHT moves agent +6 cols to ~col 50-51')

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 5, level 1.
Valid actions right now: LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - **Level 1 maze**: Large sky blue region forms corridors. Agent (blue 'b', rows 37-40, cols 20-21) is in the lower corridor. Yellow dot (Y) at col 22 adjacent to agent. - **Green blocks (N)**: 7 blocks total - 3 at top-left (rows 1-5) and 4 at middle-right (rows 13-17). These are likely targets. - **Maze structure**: Upper corridor (rows 19-29, full width), narrow right passage (rows 30-36, cols 48-53), lower corridor (rows 37-62, full width). - **Goal hypothesis**: Navigate agent to collect/reach green blocks.
- Plan: Move right to reach the passage, then find how to go up.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 4 of about 21 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The view has changed significantly! The board appears to have scrolled or the camera moved. I can now see:
- Green blocks (N) at the top-left area
- The agent is now in a different position relative to the visible area
- There's a sky blue corridor structure
Let me check the agent's actual position to understand where it is now.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check agent position after 3 RIGHT moves
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
agent = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'b'][0]
yellow = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'Y'][0]

print(f"Agent boundary: {agent['boundary']}")
print(f"Yellow boundary: {yellow['boundary']}")

# Check the area around agent
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\nAround agent:")
for r in range(36, 43):
    print(f"Row {r}: {lines[r][40:56]}")

# Check if the board scrolled or agent moved
print(f"\nPrediction: {last_prediction_result}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:

Yellow boundary: [[38, 46], [39, 46]]

Row 38: SSSBbbYBSSSSSSBB

Row 41: SSSSBBBSSSSSSSBB
Row 42: SSSSSSSSSSSSSSBB

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The agent moved from cols 26-27 to cols 44-45. That's +18 columns (3 moves × 6 columns each). The yellow dot is at col 46.
Now the agent is near the right side of the corridor. The passage to go up is at cols 48-53, rows 30-36. The agent is at col 44-45, so it needs to move right a bit more to reach the passage.
But wait - I need to figure out how to go UP. The valid actions are LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7. There's no UP/DOWN. Maybe MOUSE or ACTION7 is needed to go up?
Let me look at the passage area more carefully. The narrow passage is at cols 48-53, rows 30-36. Let me check what's there.
Actually, looking at the ascii output, the agent is at row 37-40, col 44-45. The passage above is at rows 30-36, cols 48-53. I need to get the agent to that passage and then figure out how to move up.
Let me try moving right once more to get closer to the passage, then try ACTION7 or MOUSE to go up.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model updated:
- **Agent**: Now at cols 44-45, row 37-40. Yellow at col 46. Each RIGHT = +6 columns.
- **Passage**: Narrow vertical passage at cols 48-53, rows 30-36 connects lower to upper corridors.
- **Missing**: No UP/DOWN actions. Need to discover how to move vertically (MOUSE or ACTION7?).
Plan: Move right once more to align with passage, then try ACTION7 or MOUSE to go up.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move right once more to align with passage at cols 48-53
# Agent at col 44-45, need to get to ~col 48-50
predict(expect_change=True, note='RIGHT moves agent +6 cols to ~col 50-51')
action(['RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
